title: Types of business entities
summary: The legal form a business takes shapes its risk, its finance and its control long before it makes a single sale. This lesson works through the whole of syllabus 1.2: the private-sector entities — sole traders, partnerships, privately held companies (Ltd) and publicly held companies (plc) — with their features, advantages and disadvantages; the difference between limited and unlimited liability; incorporation and the divorce of ownership from control; for-profit social enterprises (cooperatives, microfinance providers, public–private partnerships) and non-profit social enterprises (NGOs and charities); and how to choose an appropriate legal structure. Every worked example is set out exactly the way our marking engine awards Business Management marks — AO1 knowledge, AO2 application to context and AO3 analysis and evaluation — so you can see how a supported judgement, not a memorised list, is what earns the top band.
learningObjectives: Distinguish between sole traders, partnerships, privately held companies (Ltd) and publicly held companies (plc), and state the features, advantages and disadvantages of each.
Explain the difference between limited and unlimited liability and why it drives the decision to incorporate.
Explain incorporation and the divorce of ownership from control that separate legal identity creates.
Distinguish for-profit social enterprises (cooperatives, microfinance providers, public–private partnerships) from non-profit social enterprises (NGOs and charities).
Recommend an appropriate legal structure for a business by applying the concepts to context and reaching a supported judgement.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Choosing your business's legal skin
simpleExplanation.summary: A legal structure is the skin a business lives in. It decides who owns the business, who is on the hook for its debts, how easily it can raise money and how much control the founders keep. Pick the wrong skin and you are either exposed to risks you cannot afford or wrapped in paperwork you do not need.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of starting out as walking around in your own clothes: quick, cheap, and every scrape lands on your own skin — that is a sole trader or an ordinary partnership with unlimited liability. Incorporating as a company is like stepping inside a separate legal suit that stands on its own two feet. If the suit gets sued, only the suit's assets are at risk, not your personal savings — that is limited liability. But once the suit can be owned by lots of people who each hold a share of it, the people wearing it (the managers) may not be the people who own it (the shareholders), and now they have to answer to owners they have never met — that is the divorce of ownership from control.
steps: Decide how much personal risk you can carry. Unlimited liability puts your house and savings on the line; limited liability caps your loss at what you invested.
Work out how much finance you need and where it comes from. A sole trader leans on savings and loans; an Ltd can sell shares privately; a plc can sell shares to the public.
Decide how much control you are willing to give up. More owners and more shareholders usually means more capital but less say for the founders.
Match the structure to the mission. A profit-seeking start-up, a member-owned cooperative and a charity relieving poverty all need different legal forms.

flashcards.back: A business owned and run by one person, with no legal distinction between owner and business. Simple and cheap to set up, the owner keeps all profit and has full control — but bears unlimited liability and often struggles to raise finance.

A business owned by two or more people (typically up to 20) who share capital, expertise, profits and decisions. An ordinary partnership is unincorporated, so partners have unlimited liability and are jointly liable for the firm's debts.
The owner(s) are personally responsible for ALL the business's debts. There is no legal separation, so creditors can pursue personal assets — house, car, savings — if the business cannot pay.

The owners' losses are capped at the amount they invested. The company is a separate legal person, so personal assets are protected if it fails. This is the single biggest reason businesses incorporate.

An incorporated business with limited liability whose shares are sold privately to invited investors (family, friends, business angels) and NOT traded on a stock exchange. Ownership stays with a small group.

An incorporated business with limited liability that can sell shares to the general public on a stock exchange. This raises large amounts of capital but brings public disclosure, regulation and the risk of takeover.

The legal process of creating a company as a separate legal entity from its owners. The company can own assets, sign contracts, sue and be sued in its own name — and this separate identity is what grants limited liability.

When a company's owners (shareholders) are different people from those who run it (directors and managers), ownership is separated from control. This can create a conflict of objectives — an example of a principal–agent problem.

A revenue-generating business that pursues a social or environmental mission alongside profit, reinvesting most surplus into that mission. Includes cooperatives, microfinance providers and public–private partnerships.

A for-profit social enterprise owned and democratically controlled by its members (workers, customers or producers), who share the surplus. Each member usually has one vote regardless of investment size.
An organisation offering small loans and financial services to low-income individuals who lack access to conventional banks, aiming to reduce poverty by funding self-employment and small enterprises.
A venture in which a government body and a private-sector firm jointly finance, build or run a project (such as a hospital or road), combining public purpose with private capital and expertise.
An organisation whose primary aim is a social mission, not profit; any surplus is reinvested and never distributed to owners. Includes non-governmental organisations (NGOs) and charities.

A type of non-profit organisation with legally recognised charitable purposes (e.g. relief of poverty, advancement of education) operating for public benefit. Charities rely on donations, grants and trading, and often receive tax advantages.

A privately held company (Ltd) is not necessarily small, and a publicly held company (plc) is NOT government-owned. The difference is who may buy the shares — a private circle versus the general public on a stock exchange.
Incorporation just means becoming a separate legal entity with limited liability — every Ltd is incorporated. It does NOT mean floating shares on a stock exchange; only a plc does that.

quickCheck.prompt: A business fails owing $60,000 it cannot pay. In which structure can creditors legally pursue the owner's personal house and savings?
options: A sole trader, because the owner has unlimited liability
A privately held company (Ltd), because its shares are private
A publicly held company (plc), because it is listed
Any incorporated company, because incorporation removes protection
quickCheck.answer: A sole trader, because the owner has unlimited liability

quickCheck.prompt: What is the key legal difference between a privately held company (Ltd) and a publicly held company (plc)?
options: An Ltd has limited liability but a plc does not
A plc can sell shares to the general public on a stock exchange; an Ltd sells shares only privately
An Ltd is owned by the government; a plc is owned by individuals
A plc is unincorporated while an Ltd is incorporated
quickCheck.answer: A plc can sell shares to the general public on a stock exchange; an Ltd sells shares only privately

quickCheck.prompt: Which statement best describes what incorporation achieves?
options: It guarantees the business will make a profit
It floats the company's shares on a stock exchange
It creates a separate legal entity from the owners, giving them limited liability
It converts a company into a non-profit organisation
quickCheck.answer: It creates a separate legal entity from the owners, giving them limited liability

quickCheck.prompt: A member-owned coffee-growers' organisation sells beans, makes a surplus and shares it democratically among its farmer members. This is best classified as:
options: A charity, because it helps farmers
A non-profit NGO, because it has a social aim
A for-profit social enterprise (a cooperative)
A publicly held company, because it sells a product

quickCheck.prompt: Which is the correct distinction between a charity and a for-profit social enterprise?
options: A charity never generates any revenue; a social enterprise never has a social aim
A charity's primary aim is its social mission and it distributes no profit to owners; a for-profit social enterprise pursues a social mission AND profit
They are the same thing under different names
A charity has shareholders; a social enterprise does not
quickCheck.answer: A charity's primary aim is its social mission and it distributes no profit to owners; a for-profit social enterprise pursues a social mission AND profit

faq.q: Is a publicly held company (plc) owned by the government?
faq.a: No — this is one of the most common misconceptions. A plc is owned by whichever members of the public buy its shares on a stock exchange. 'Public' means the public may own a piece of it, not that the state owns it. A business owned by the government is a state-owned enterprise, which is a public-SECTOR organisation and a different idea entirely.

faq.q: Can a sole trader employ staff?
faq.a: Yes. 'Sole trader' describes the ownership — one owner — not the number of people working there. A sole trader can hire employees, but remains the single owner, keeps the profit and, crucially, still carries unlimited liability for everything the business owes.

faq.q: What exactly is the 'divorce of ownership from control'?
faq.a: In a sole trader or small partnership the owners also run the business, so ownership and control sit with the same people. Once a company sells shares to outside investors, the owners (shareholders) may take no part in day-to-day management, which is handled by directors and managers. Ownership is then divorced from control. Because managers may pursue their own goals (status, growth, salary) rather than the shareholders' goal of returns, this can create a principal–agent conflict of objectives.

No — and the syllabus is precise here. For-profit social enterprises (cooperatives, microfinance providers, public–private partnerships) deliberately earn a profit but tie it to a social mission and reinvest most of the surplus. Non-profit social enterprises (NGOs and charities) exist for the mission alone and distribute no profit to owners at all. The dividing line is not whether they make money, but whether profit is a genuine objective and whether any surplus can be paid out to owners.

faq.q: How do I decide which structure a case-study business should choose?
faq.a: Weigh four factors against the specific business: the owners' appetite for risk (unlimited vs limited liability), how much finance is needed and from where, how much control the founders want to keep, and the underlying aim (profit, member benefit or social mission). There is no single 'best' structure — the marks come from applying these factors to the given context and justifying a choice, not from reciting a definition.

sections.content: Before a business hires a single employee or sells a single product, its founders make a decision that shapes everything that follows: what legal form it will take. That one choice fixes who owns the business, who is personally liable if it collapses, how it can raise money, and how much control the founders keep. This lesson covers the whole of syllabus 1.2 — the private-sector entities (sole traders, partnerships, Ltds and plcs), the pivotal difference between limited and unlimited liability, incorporation and the divorce of ownership from control, for-profit and non-profit social enterprises, and how to choose an appropriate structure. Because 1.2 is an evaluation-heavy topic, the worked examples are set out the way our marking engine actually awards marks, so you can see why a supported judgement outscores a memorised list every time.
The private sector and the unincorporated forms

Private-sector organisations are owned by individuals rather than the state, and most exist to earn profit for their owners. The simplest private-sector forms are unincorporated: the law draws no line between the owner and the business, so the business is not a separate legal person. That makes these forms cheap and fast to start, but it also means the owners carry unlimited liability — they are personally responsible for every debt the business runs up. Two forms dominate: the sole trader and the partnership.

items: **Sole trader:** one owner who is the business in law. Features: full control, keeps all profit, minimal set-up cost and privacy of accounts. Advantages: fast to start, quick decisions, strong personal incentive. Disadvantages: unlimited liability, limited access to finance, heavy workload and no continuity if the owner dies.

**Partnership:** two or more owners (usually up to 20) sharing capital, skills, profits and decisions, often governed by a partnership agreement (deed). Advantages: more capital and expertise than a sole trader, shared workload and shared risk. Disadvantages: ordinary partners have unlimited liability and are jointly liable, profits are shared, and disputes can arise over decisions.

**Unlimited liability:** the defining drawback of both forms — because owner and business are one in law, creditors can seize personal assets to settle business debts. This is the risk that pushes many growing firms toward incorporation.

sections.question: Anisha runs a one-person graphic-design business as a sole trader. Explain one advantage and one disadvantage to Anisha of operating as a sole trader. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
One advantage is that Anisha has complete control and keeps all the profit her business makes. As a sole trader she is the only owner, so she can make design and pricing decisions instantly without consulting partners or shareholders, and every dollar of profit is hers — a strong personal incentive to work hard and grow the business.

One disadvantage is unlimited liability. Because there is no legal separation between Anisha and her business, if the business ran up debts it could not pay — for example after taking a loan to buy equipment — creditors could pursue her personal assets such as her savings or home. This exposes her personally to the full downside of any failure.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — the command term 'explain' calls for AO2 application, so each point must be developed and tied to Anisha, not left generic:

- **AO1 (1):** identifies a valid advantage — full control / keeps all profit.
- **AO2 (1):** develops it in Anisha's context — makes fast design/pricing decisions alone; profit is her personal income.
- **AO1 (1):** identifies a valid disadvantage — unlimited liability.
- **AO2 (1):** develops it in context — no legal separation, so her personal savings/home are at risk if the design business cannot pay its debts.

The engine credits developed points, not bare terms: writing only 'unlimited liability' without explaining what it means for Anisha's personal assets caps the point at the AO1 mark and forfeits the AO2 application mark.

sections.content: Limited liability and incorporation

Incorporation is the legal process of turning a business into a company — a separate legal entity that exists in its own right, distinct from the people who own it. Once incorporated, the company can own property, sign contracts, borrow, sue and be sued in its own name. The decisive consequence is limited liability: because the company, not the owner, owns the debts, an owner's loss is capped at the amount they invested in shares. If the company fails, shareholders lose their investment but their personal assets are protected. Limited liability is the single greatest advantage of incorporation, and it is why owners accept the extra cost and disclosure that come with it. A second consequence follows once shares are sold to outsiders: the divorce of ownership from control.

items: **Incorporation:** creates a separate legal entity; the business becomes a 'legal person' distinct from its owners.
**Limited liability:** each owner's loss is capped at the value of their shares; personal assets are shielded from company debts.
**Trade-offs of incorporating:** company accounts must be filed and are publicly visible, set-up and compliance cost more, and decisions can be slower — the price of the protection.
**Continuity:** unlike a sole trader, a company continues to exist when owners sell their shares or die — it has perpetual succession.

sections.content: Privately held (Ltd) and publicly held (plc) companies

Both an Ltd and a plc are incorporated companies with limited liability, owned by shareholders. What separates them is who is allowed to buy the shares. A privately held company (Ltd) sells its shares privately, to a chosen circle of family, friends and invited investors; the shares cannot be offered to the general public. This keeps ownership and control within a small group but limits how much capital can be raised. A publicly held company (plc) may offer its shares to the general public on a stock exchange, which unlocks very large amounts of capital for expansion — but at the cost of full public disclosure of performance, heavy regulation, pressure to satisfy shareholders each period, and vulnerability to hostile takeover if enough shares are bought.

items: **Privately held company (Ltd):** shares sold privately; ownership stays with a small, known group; limited liability; accounts filed but capital-raising is capped. Suits owners who want protection without losing control.

**Publicly held company (plc):** shares traded publicly on a stock exchange; can raise large capital for growth; faces full disclosure, regulatory cost, short-term shareholder pressure and takeover risk.
**The bright line:** private = shares to an invited circle only; public = shares to anyone via the stock market. 'Public' does NOT mean government-owned.
**Ownership vs control:** as a firm moves from Ltd toward plc and sells more shares, the founders' control dilutes and ownership separates further from management.

sections.content: Anchor every structure choice to the case study's own details. If a business needs a large amount of capital to expand nationally, that points toward a plc; if the owners are risk-averse and want to protect their family home while keeping control, an Ltd beats a sole trader. Use the syllabus vocabulary — 'limited liability', 'access to finance', 'control', 'incorporation' — and always say what it means for THIS business, because the application marks live in that link.
The divorce of ownership from control

In a sole trader or a small partnership the owners run the business themselves, so ownership and control sit in the same hands. Once a company sells shares to investors who play no part in day-to-day management, the picture splits: the shareholders own the business, but directors and managers control it. This separation is the divorce of ownership from control. It brings a benefit — professional managers can run the firm while owners simply supply capital — but also a risk: managers may pursue their own aims, such as growth, status or higher salaries, rather than the shareholders' aim of strong returns. That conflict of objectives between owners (principals) and managers (agents) is why plcs invest in corporate governance, boards and incentives to keep managers acting in owners' interests.
Social enterprises: for-profit and non-profit

Not every organisation exists to maximise owner profit. Social enterprises put a social, ethical or environmental mission at their centre. The syllabus splits them in two by how they treat profit. For-profit social enterprises deliberately earn a profit but bind it to a social purpose, reinvesting most of the surplus into the mission or community rather than paying it all out to owners. Non-profit social enterprises exist for the mission alone and distribute no profit to owners — any surplus is reinvested. Getting this two-way split right, with the correct examples on each side, is exactly what 1.2 questions test.

items: **For-profit social enterprises** — earn a profit tied to a social mission, surplus mostly reinvested:
• **Cooperatives:** owned and democratically controlled by their members (workers, consumers or producers), who share the surplus; typically one member, one vote.
• **Microfinance providers:** offer small loans and financial services to low-income people excluded from mainstream banks, funding self-employment and reducing poverty.
• **Public–private partnerships (PPPs):** government and a private firm jointly finance or run a project (e.g. a hospital or motorway), combining public purpose with private capital and expertise.
**Non-profit social enterprises** — exist for the mission, distribute no profit to owners:
• **NGOs (non-governmental organisations):** private, independent bodies pursuing social or development aims, funded by grants, donations and some trading.
• **Charities:** NGOs with legally recognised charitable purposes operating for public benefit, funded by donations, grants and trading, and often receiving tax advantages.

sections.content: Choosing an appropriate legal structure

There is no universally 'best' legal structure — the right one depends on the business in front of you. Four questions do most of the work. First, how much personal risk can the owners bear? If failure would ruin them, limited liability (incorporation) matters. Second, how much finance is needed and where from? Savings and loans suit a sole trader; private share sales suit an Ltd; only a plc can tap the public markets for large sums. Third, how much control do the founders want to keep? Every new owner or shareholder dilutes it. Fourth, what is the underlying aim — private profit, member benefit, or a social mission? A profit-seeking start-up, a farmer cooperative and a poverty charity each need a different form. A strong exam answer weighs these factors against the specific context and commits to a justified recommendation.

items: **Liability and risk:** the higher the debts and the greater the owners' exposure, the stronger the case for incorporation and limited liability.
**Finance needs:** small and internal → sole trader/partnership; moderate and private → Ltd; large and public → plc.
**Control:** founders wanting to keep control favour a sole trader or Ltd over a plc; selling shares trades control for capital.
**Aim of the organisation:** profit → company; member benefit/social mission with surplus → cooperative or other social enterprise; mission with no distributed profit → NGO or charity.
**Judgement:** the choice is a balance of these factors for the given business — state which factor is decisive here and why.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Confusing limited and unlimited liability** — sole traders and ordinary partners have UNLIMITED liability (personal assets at risk); only incorporated companies give owners LIMITED liability. Reversing this undermines the whole answer.

**Thinking a plc is government-owned** — 'public' means the public can buy its shares on a stock exchange, not that the state owns it. A state-owned business is a public-SECTOR organisation, a different concept.
**Assuming an Ltd is always small and a plc always large** — the labels are about who may buy the shares (private circle vs general public), not about size.

**Treating incorporation as the same as 'going public'** — incorporation just means becoming a separate legal entity with limited liability (every Ltd is incorporated). Floating shares on a stock exchange is a further step only a plc takes.

**Mislabelling social enterprises** — a member-owned, surplus-sharing cooperative is a FOR-PROFIT social enterprise, not a charity; a charity distributes no profit and exists for its mission. Put the right example on the right side.
**Listing advantages and disadvantages without applying them** — a bare list earns AO1 only; the marks climb when each point is tied to the specific business in the case.
**Recommending without a supported judgement** — an 'evaluate' or 'recommend' answer that gives both sides but never commits to a justified conclusion cannot reach the top band.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

Business Management 1.2 is assessed against three objectives: AO1 rewards relevant knowledge and understanding, AO2 rewards applying that knowledge to the specific business in the stimulus, and AO3 rewards analysis and a balanced evaluation. In the analytic/points scheme each distinct valid point earns credit, but the higher 'evaluate' and 'recommend' marks are reserved for answers that combine APPLICATION to context with a BALANCED evaluation that ends in a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT. Watch how the marks below attach to applied, two-sided reasoning and a justified conclusion — never to a generic list.

sections.question: Rahul runs a fast-growing artisan bakery as a sole trader. Sales have tripled in two years and he now wants to open three more branches, which needs significant investment and would leave him with large debts. Recommend whether Rahul should change his bakery from a sole trader to a privately held company (Ltd). [10]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
As a sole trader, Rahul currently has unlimited liability, so the large debts he would take on to open three new branches would put his personal assets — his savings and home — directly at risk if the expansion failed. Incorporating as a privately held company (Ltd) would create a separate legal entity and give him limited liability, capping his personal loss at what he invests. Given that the expansion is specifically described as leaving him with large debts, this protection is the strongest argument for the change: it lets him pursue an ambitious, risky expansion without gambling his personal wealth.

Becoming an Ltd would also improve access to finance for the three new branches. As a sole trader Rahul is largely limited to his own savings and bank loans, but an Ltd can sell shares privately to family, friends or business angels who believe in the bakery. This could raise the significant investment he needs more readily than debt alone, and may also be viewed more favourably by lenders because an incorporated company appears more established and permanent.

However, the change carries real drawbacks. Incorporation brings extra cost and administration: an Ltd must file annual accounts that become publicly visible, and Rahul loses the privacy and simplicity he enjoys as a sole trader. More importantly, if he raises the investment by selling shares, he dilutes his ownership and control — decisions he currently makes alone would have to consider other shareholders, beginning a divorce of ownership from control. For a founder used to running his bakery his own way, that loss of autonomy is a genuine cost.

There is also the question of whether the change is necessary yet. If Rahul could fund a smaller expansion from retained profit and modest borrowing, he might delay incorporating and keep full control. But that is not the scenario described: he wants three branches and would carry large debts.

**Judgement.** On balance, Rahul should change to a privately held company (Ltd). The decisive factor is the scale of the debt the expansion creates: with unlimited liability, one failed branch could cost him his home, whereas limited liability confines the risk to the business. The improved access to private share capital directly supports the three-branch plan, while the main drawbacks — extra administration and some dilution of control — are manageable, especially as an Ltd lets him keep majority ownership rather than floating shares publicly. The protection and finance outweigh the loss of simplicity for a business expanding this fast, so incorporation as an Ltd is the appropriate choice.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 10 marks:**

- **AO1 — knowledge (relevant concepts):** the engine credits accurate command of the ideas the question turns on — unlimited vs limited liability, incorporation as creating a separate legal entity, the Ltd's private share sales, and the divorce of ownership from control. Correct terminology signals AO1.
- **AO2 — application to THIS business:** marks are awarded for tying those concepts to Rahul specifically — the large expansion debts that make limited liability valuable, selling shares to fund the three branches, his personal home being at risk as a sole trader. Generic textbook points that never mention his bakery do not earn AO2.
- **AO3 — analysis and balanced evaluation:** the engine credits developed two-sided reasoning — benefits (limited liability, access to private share capital) weighed against drawbacks (administration and disclosure, dilution of control/ownership–control divorce) — and, crucially, a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT that commits to a decision and justifies WHY one side outweighs the other for this context.
- **What lifts an answer into the top band:** application plus judgement. The engine rewards an answer that applies the concepts to Rahul and ends in a reasoned recommendation; it does NOT reward a longer generic list. A response that lists five advantages and five disadvantages of Ltds but never applies them to the bakery and never decides is held in the lower bands, however much it contains.
- **What the engine rejects:** all-benefits or all-drawbacks answers with no balance; a 'judgement' that merely restates both sides without choosing; and correct definitions with no link to Rahul's expansion.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — AO1 for the right concepts, AO2 for applying them to the business in the question, and AO3 for balanced analysis ending in a supported judgement. So do not just list advantages and disadvantages: name the concept (limited liability, incorporation, ownership vs control), apply it to the specific business, weigh both sides, and finish by committing to a recommendation with a reason. The application and the judgement are where the top-band marks are won.
Where this leads

The distinctions in this topic underpin much of what follows in the course. Limited liability and the divorce of ownership from control return in stakeholder and corporate-governance analysis; the finance implications of each structure feed directly into sources of finance and the growth-and-evolution unit, where Ltds convert to plcs and firms merge or float. Master the habit built here — identify the concept, apply it to the specific business, weigh both sides, then commit to a justified judgement — and you have the template that earns marks across every evaluation question in Business Management.
